<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Rapid Tooling. List the two primary classifications of Rapid Tooling as per Gibson's framework.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Rapid Tooling (RT) is the use of additive manufacturing technologies to produce tooling components such as molds, dies, patterns, and inserts in a significantly reduced lead time compared to conventional tool-making.

As per **Gibson's classification (1997)**, RT is broadly classified into:
1. **Direct Rapid Tooling** — where the final functional tool is fabricated directly by the AM process.
2. **Indirect Rapid Tooling** — where AM is used to fabricate a pattern or master, which is then used in conventional processes to produce the final tool.

*(Award 1 mark each for the two classifications and 1 mark for the definition.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Bridge Tooling and Hard Tooling with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Parameter | Bridge Tooling | Hard Tooling |
|---|---|---|
| **Material** | Aluminum, epoxy, 3D-printed polymer | Hardened tool steel (H13, P20, S7) |
| **Service Life** | 100–5,000 parts | >100,000 parts |
| **Lead Time** | 1–7 days | 8–16 weeks |
| **Application** | Prototype / market-test runs | Mass production |
| **Example** | SLS nylon mold for first 2,000 parts of a consumer product | DMLS H13 insert with full hardening for automotive dashboard mold |

*(Award 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for material, 1 mark for example.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 scheme, Part B allows an internal choice between two questions on the same module. Both alternatives are given below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Classify Rapid Tooling in detail. Explain **Direct Rapid Tooling** with its sub-categories, suitable AM processes, tool materials, and typical production volumes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **process flow of Direct Metal Tooling via DMLS** for an injection-mold insert made of H13 tool steel. Include all pre-process, in-process, and post-process steps. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Rapid Tooling (7 marks):**

Rapid Tooling is classified into **two primary families** based on Gibson's framework, and **three functional classes** based on tool life.

**Primary Classification:**

1. **Direct Rapid Tooling** – The final tool is directly 3D-printed.
   - *Direct Metal Tooling* (DMLS/SLM/EBM) → H13, P20, Maraging steel → 5,000–100,000 parts.
   - *Direct Polymer Tooling* (SLS/SLA/FDM) → Polyamide, Somos resin, ULTEM → 100–5,000 parts.
   - *Direct Sand Moulds* (3DP Binder Jetting) → Silica sand + binder → 1–500 castings.

2. **Indirect Rapid Tooling** – AM is used to print an intermediate pattern.
   - *Sacrificial Pattern* for investment casting (SLA/SLS/FDM).
   - *Master Pattern* for silicone/RTV vacuum casting (SLA/PolyJet).
   - *Sand Mould Pattern* (FDM/3DP).
   - *Ancillary Tool Parts* (sprue bushes, baffles).

**Functional Classification (by tool life):**
1. **Bridge Tooling** (100–5K parts) — Aluminum, epoxy, 3D polymer.
2. **Soft Tooling** (5K–50K parts) — Aluminum, Kirksite, AM metal.
3. **Hard Tooling** (>100K parts) — Hardened H13, P20, S7 steel.

*(Award 2 marks for primary classification, 2 marks for sub-categories, 2 marks for functional classes, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

**(b) Process Flow: DMLS of H13 Injection-Mold Insert (7 marks):**

| Step | Operation | Key Parameter / Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | CAD design of negative cavity + conformal cooling channels | STL file, layer thickness 30 µm |
| 2 | Build platform pre-heat, N₂ purge | O₂ < 0.1% |
| 3 | Powder spreading (gas-atomized H13, 20–40 µm) | Recoater layer |
| 4 | Yb-fiber laser selective melting | P = 200 W, v = 800 mm/s, h = 0.12 mm |
| 5 | VED control | VED = 60–100 J/mm³ |
| 6 | Layer-by-layer build | N = H_part / t layers |
| 7 | Stress relief (650°C, 2 h, furnace cool) | Residual stress <50 MPa |
| 8 | Depowdering, Wire-EDM platform separation | Part separated |
| 9 | CNC finish-machining of critical surfaces | Ra 0.2–0.8 µm, ±0.01 mm |
| 10 | Vacuum hardening (1020°C) + triple temper (550°C) | HRC 54–58 |
| 11 | Insert fit into standard mold base + trial shot | Production ready |

**Key Equations:**
$$
\text{VED} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \left[ \text{J/mm}^3 \right]
$$
$$
N = \frac{H_{\text{part}}}{t}
$$

*(Award 1 mark per correctly explained step. Deduct 1 mark if stress relief (Step 7) is missing.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Indirect Rapid Tooling** route using **Sacrificial Investment Casting Patterns**. Describe the complete process chain from CAD to final investment-cast tool. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the four RT functional classes (Bridge, Soft, Hard, Prototype) with respect to **tool material, lead time, production volume, and cost per part**. Identify the RT class best suited for a **production run of 30,000 plastic injection-molded parts** and justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Indirect RT — Sacrificial Investment Casting Pattern Route (7 marks):**

1. **CAD Design of the tool insert** (negative cavity) → STL conversion.
2. **AM of sacrificial pattern** in castable photopolymer (SLA) or polymer (FDM).
3. **Pattern cleaning & assembly** with gating system, sprue, runner.
4. **Investment (shell building):**
   - Dip pattern in ceramic slurry → drain → apply stucco.
   - Repeat **6–8 times** → shell thickness **8–12 mm**.
   - Drying between each coat (2–4 h air-dry).
5. **Dewaxing (autoclave):** Steam at **200°C, 0.7 MPa, 8 min** → pattern melts out.
6. **High-Temperature Burnout:** **1100°C, 2 h** in furnace to remove residue and sinter ceramic shell.
7. **Metal Pouring:** Molten tool steel (H13) or aluminum at **1500–1600°C** (steel).
8. **Solidification & Knockout:** Cool in air, break ceramic shell, retrieve casting.
9. **Cutoff, grind, finish-machine, heat-treat** the final tool.
10. **Final tool** is ready for production with full metallurgical properties.

**Key advantages over Direct RT:** Better surface finish (Ra 0.8–1.6 µm as-cast), full heat treatment possible, no AM-induced residual stresses.

*(Award 1 mark for each major step. Mention at least one temperature parameter to score full marks.)*

---

**(b) Comparison of RT Functional Classes (7 marks):**

| Class | Tool Material | Lead Time | Volume (parts) | Cost / Part |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Prototype** | Polymer, silicone, 3DP sand | 0.5–2 days | ≤ 50 | Very High (₹ 5000+) |
| **Bridge** | Aluminum, epoxy, 3D polymer | 1–5 days | 100–5,000 | High (₹ 200–2000) |
| **Soft** | Aluminum, Kirksite, DMLS H13 | 3–7 days | 5,000–50,000 | Medium (₹ 50–200) |
| **Hard** | Hardened tool steel | 8–16 weeks | >100,000 | Low (₹ 5–30) |

**Cost-per-part logic:**
$$
C_{\text{part}} = \frac{C_{\text{tool}}}{N} + C_{\text{material}} + C_{\text{machining}}
$$

For a **30,000-part run of injection-molded plastic parts**, the optimal choice is **Soft Tooling (DMLS H13 insert with conventional mold base)**.

**Justification (3 reasons):**
1. **Volume is in the upper edge of the soft-tooling band** (5K–50K), and using hard tooling would require 8–16 weeks of lead time, which is unjustified for a 30K run.
2. **DMLS H13 insert (with conformal cooling channels) provides a service life of 30,000–50,000 parts** at HRC 50–54, sufficient for the run.
3. **Cost analysis:** A DMLS H13 insert costs approx. ₹ 1–3 Lakh, vs. ₹ 15–30 Lakh for fully hardened conventional tool steel. The savings are substantial for a 30K run.

*(Award 1 mark for the comparison table, 1 mark for the cost formula, 1 mark for selection, 2 marks for justification, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Rapid Tooling" with "Rapid Prototyping":** Many students use the terms interchangeably. Remember: **Rapid Prototyping = making the part**; **Rapid Tooling = making the tool to make the part.**
> 
> 2. **Omitting "Indirect Tooling" sub-categories:** A 14-mark question often demands *all four* sub-categories of Indirect RT (sacrificial, master, sand pattern, ancillary). Missing any one costs 1–2 marks.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the conformal-cooling advantage** of Direct Metal Tooling. This is the **most-likely "Why" question** in 14-mark papers.
> 
> 4. **Not mentioning the VED formula** while explaining DMLS — even though this is a classification question, examiners expect a quantitative anchor.
> 
> 5. **Mixing up Bridge vs. Soft Tooling volumes** (Bridge = 100–5K; Soft = 5K–50K). This is a *favourite 3-mark trap*.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Rapid Tooling = making the TOOL fast, not the part.** This is the foundational distinction.
- ✅ **Two primary classes (Gibson):** **Direct RT** (final tool printed directly) and **Indirect RT** (pattern printed, then conventional tool made).
- ✅ **Three functional classes (by life):** **Bridge (100–5K), Soft (5K–50K), Hard (>100K).**
- ✅ **Direct Metal Tooling** uses **DMLS/SLM/EBM** with **H13, P20, Maraging steel, 316L**.
- ✅ **Direct Polymer Tooling** uses **SLS/SLA/FDM** with **Polyamide, Somos, ULTEM**.
- ✅ **Direct Sand Moulds** use **3DP Binder Jetting** (ExOne, Voxeljet).
- ✅ **Indirect Sacrificial Pattern** = SLA/SLS/FDM pattern → investment casting (best surface finish, full metallurgical quality).
- ✅ **Indirect Master Pattern** = SLA/PolyJet master → silicone/RTV mold (10–200 parts).
- ✅ **Conformal cooling channels** (possible only with AM) reduce cycle time by **30–60%**.
- ✅ **VED formula:** $ \text{VED} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} $ — **J/mm³** (H13 optimum: 60–100 J/mm³).
- ✅ **Mandatory post-processes for DMLS H13:** Stress relief (650°C/2h), CNC finishing, vacuum hardening (1020°C), triple tempering (550°C) → **HRC 54–58**.
- ✅ **Cost per part formula:** $ C_{\text{part}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{tool}}}{N} + C_{\text{material}} + C_{\text{machining}} $.
- ✅ **Investment casting burnout parameters:** Autoclave dewax at **200°C, 0.7 MPa, 8 min**; furnace burnout at **1100°C, 2 h**.
- ✅ **Lead-Time Compression Factor (LTCF):** $ \text{LTCF} = \dfrac{T_{\text{conventional}}}{T_{\text{rapid}}} $ — must be **≥ 4** to justify RT economically.
- ✅ **Quick mnemonic: "D-I-S-H"** = Direct, Indirect, Soft, Hard — covers all four classes.
- ✅ **For 30,000-part plastic injection run** → **Soft Tooling (DMLS H13)** is the optimal answer.
- ✅ **ASTM/ISO terminology:** Direct RT is also called **"AM Tooling"**; Indirect RT is called **"Pattern-Based Tooling"** in newer standards.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct and Indirect Tooling Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT524 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING AND 3D PRINTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Direct and Indirect Tooling Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tooling in Additive Manufacturing (AM)** refers to the use of AM processes to manufacture tools, dies, molds, jigs, and fixtures used in conventional manufacturing operations such as injection molding, die casting, thermoforming, and stamping. Based on the manufacturing route adopted to obtain the final usable tool, tooling is broadly classified into two categories: **Direct Tooling** and **Indirect Tooling**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct Tooling:** A Rapid Tooling (RT) approach in which the *final, production-ready tool* is fabricated directly using an Additive Manufacturing process, without any subsequent conventional forming, casting, or molding operations. The AM-built part is the actual tool that goes into the production machine (e.g., an injection molding machine or a die casting press).

> [!NOTE]
> **Indirect Tooling:** A Rapid Tooling approach in which AM is used to fabricate an *intermediate pattern, master, or sacrificial model*, which is then used in a *secondary conventional process* (such as investment casting, sand casting, or ceramic shell building) to produce the final metallic or composite tool. The AM part is *not* the tool itself — it is a step in the tool-making chain.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you need a metal cookie cutter shaped like a star.

- **Direct Tooling analogy:** You 3D-print the cookie cutter *itself* in metal (using SLM/DMLS) and use it directly to cut cookies. The printed object = the tool. 🍪✦
- **Indirect Tooling analogy:** You 3D-print a *plastic star* (the pattern), then dip it in ceramic slurry to form a mold, burn out the plastic, pour molten metal into the cavity, and finally get a metal cookie cutter. The printed object ≠ the tool, but a *means* to make the tool. ⭐🔥

**Key Distinction at a glance:**

| Aspect | Direct Tooling | Indirect Tooling |
|---|---|---|
| **What is 3D printed?** | The final tool itself | An intermediate pattern/master |
| **Secondary process?** | ❌ Not required | ✅ Required (casting, molding) |
| **Tool material** | Same as AM material (metal, polymer, sand) | Conventional tool material (steel, aluminum) |
| **Lead time** | Very short (hours to days) | Moderate (days to weeks) |
| **Tool life** | Limited to a few hundred to a few thousand cycles | Comparable to conventional tools (tens of thousands of cycles) |
| **Cost** | Lower upfront | Lower per-part at high volumes |

---

## 1.3 Soft Tooling vs. Hard Tooling — A Critical Pre-Requisite

Before diving into the two methods, the KTU syllabus demands understanding this foundational dichotomy:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soft Tooling:** Tools made from *low-melting-point alloys, polymers, reinforced plastics, or filled resins* — meant for **short production runs** (prototyping and bridge production). Examples: 3D-printed polymer injection molds, silicone thermoforming tools.
>
> **Hard Tooling:** Tools made from *hardened tool steels, carbides, or pre-hardened metals* — meant for **long production runs** (mass production). Examples: SLM-printed H13 steel injection mold inserts, DMLS conformal-cooled die-casting dies.

Both Direct and Indirect methods can produce *either* soft or hard tooling depending on the material and process.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Valuation

- **Build Volume:** Typically **250 mm × 250 mm × 250 mm** for commercial SLM systems; up to **500 mm × 500 mm × 500 mm** for industrial DMLS.
- **Layer thickness:** **20 µm to 100 µm** (metals), **16 µm to 32 µm** (polymers via SLA).
- **Tool lead time reduction:** Conventional tooling ≈ **4–12 weeks**; AM tooling can reduce this to **1–7 days**.
- **Cost reduction:** **50–80%** for low-volume (≤ 1000 parts) production.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Direct Tooling — Operational Logic

Direct Tooling bypasses all secondary forming steps. The AM process itself deposits, cures, or sinters the tool material layer by layer until the geometrically complete tool is obtained.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic:

1. **CAD Tool Design:** The tool geometry (e.g., injection mold cavity and core) is modeled in CAD. Conformal cooling channels (if any) are designed as internal 3D channels following the contour of the cavity surface.
2. **Slicing & Path Planning:** The CAD model is sliced into 2D layers (typical thickness **20–50 µm** for metals). The slicer generates scan vectors and contour paths.
3. **Build Process:** Powder-bed fusion (SLM/DMLS), directed energy deposition (DED), binder jetting, or polymer-based AM (SLA, FDM) deposits material layer by layer.
4. **Stress Relief & Support Removal:** The as-built tool is heat-treated (e.g., stress relief at **600–700 °C** for tool steels) to reduce residual stresses, and support structures are machined off.
5. **Post-Processing:** CNC milling of critical sealing surfaces, EDM of fine features, polishing, and surface treatments (e.g., nitriding, PVD coating) are applied to enhance wear resistance.
6. **Installation:** The tool is fitted into the production machine (e.g., injection molding press) for production.

### Why Direct Tooling Works — The Underlying "Why"

- **Conformal cooling:** Conventional tools have straight drilled cooling channels. AM enables *conformal* channels that follow the cavity profile, reducing **cycle time by 20–40%** and eliminating warpage in molded parts.
- **Design freedom:** Internal lattices, undercuts, and complex parting lines become manufacturable in a single build, eliminating assembly of multiple tool inserts.

---

## 2.2 Indirect Tooling — Operational Logic

Indirect Tooling uses AM to create a *master pattern*, which is converted to a tool via conventional foundry or molding methods.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic:

1. **Pattern Design (CAD):** A 3D model of the *pattern* (the inverse of the tool cavity, plus shrinkage allowances) is created. Shrinkage allowance = **1.5–2.5%** for steel castings, **0.5–1%** for aluminum.
2. **AM Pattern Fabrication:** The pattern is built using:
   - **SLA / DLP** (for investment casting patterns — wax-like photopolymers)
   - **FDM / PolyJet** (for sand casting patterns — soluble or meltable polymers)
   - **SLS** (for durable patterns that can withstand ceramic shell dipping)
3. **Conventional Tool Manufacturing:** The AM pattern is used in:
   - **Sand casting** of the tool body (e.g., a sand mold is made around an FDM pattern, metal is poured, pattern is burned out or melted out).
   - **Investment casting** (lost-wax process with AM polymer replacing wax).
   - **Ceramic shell building** for near-net-shape tool inserts.
4. **Finishing:** The cast tool is machined, heat-treated, and polished to specification.

### Why Indirect Tooling Works — The Underlying "Why"

- **Material flexibility:** Conventional casting can produce tools in *any castable alloy* (H13, P20, S7 tool steels) with properties equivalent to traditional wrought tools.
- **Cost efficiency for long runs:** Although slower than direct tooling, indirect tooling yields a *durable, high-performance* tool suitable for **>100,000 production cycles**.

---

## 2.3 Direct Tooling — Process Variants (KTU-Favorite Topics)

> [!NOTE]
> The following process variants are direct tooling methods, classified by the AM technology employed:

| S.No. | Direct Tooling Method | AM Technology | Tool Material | Typical Application |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **SLM / DMLS Tooling** | Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) | Maraging steel (1.2709), H13, 316L, Inconel 625 | Injection mold inserts, die-casting cores |
| 2 | **Direct Metal Deposition (DMD/DED)** | Directed Energy Deposition | H13, copper alloys, titanium | Large die repair, conformal cooling inserts |
| 3 | **Polymer Direct Tooling (SLA/FDM)** | Vat Photopolymerization / Material Extrusion | Photopolymer resin, ABS, polycarbonate | Short-run plastic injection molds, thermoforming tools |
| 4 | **Sand Printing Direct Tooling (Voxeljet, ExOne)** | Binder Jetting | Silica sand + phenolic binder | Sand cores and molds for foundries (direct use as tooling) |
| 5 | **ProMetal (Indirect → sometimes called direct hybrid)** | Binder Jetting | Metal-binder green part → sintered | Functional metal prototypes, low-volume tooling |

---

## 2.4 Indirect Tooling — Process Variants

| S.No. | Indirect Tooling Method | Master Pattern AM Process | Secondary Process | Final Tool Material |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **QuickCast (3D Systems)** | SLA (burnout photopolymer) | Investment casting | Aluminum, tool steel |
| 2 | **FDM Pattern + Sand Casting** | FDM (ABS pattern) | Sand casting with burnout | Cast iron, steel |
| 3 | **SLS Master for Investment Casting** | SLS (polyamide pattern) | Investment casting | Superalloys, titanium |
| 4 | **PolyJet Wax Pattern** | PolyJet (castable photopolymer) | Investment casting | Stainless steel, brass |
| 5 | **Bridge Tooling (e.g., 3D Keltool)** | SLA pattern + ceramic shell | Sintering of metal powder | Tungsten carbide, tool steel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction:** *"QuickCast is an Indirect Tooling method, NOT Direct Tooling."* Even though QuickCast is a proprietary 3D Systems technology, it uses SLA to build a *pattern* that is then invested and cast. The SLA build alone is not the tool — the *cast metal* is the tool.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are tested in KTU ESE numerical/analytical questions. Memorize the *symbols* and *units* — partial credit in ESE often hinges on correctly writing units.

| Formula | Symbol Legend | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| $L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | $t_i$ = layer thickness, $n$ = number of layers | Total build height of a tool |
| $V_{build} = L \times W \times H$ | $L, W, H$ = build envelope dimensions | Maximum tool size in a single build |
| $\Delta t_{cycle} = t_{conv} - t_{conf}$ | Conformal cooling cycle time reduction | Quantifying AM cooling benefit |
| $\eta_{cool} = \dfrac{Q_{removed}}{Q_{input}}$ | Cooling efficiency of channels | Comparison of straight vs. conformal channels |
| $L_{pattern} = L_{tool}(1 + S)$ | $S$ = total shrinkage factor (casting + thermal) | Pattern dimensioning for indirect tooling |
| $S_{total} = S_{cast} + S_{thermal} + S_{pattern}$ | Combined shrinkage compensation | KTU common 3-mark question |
| $C_{per\_part} = \dfrac{C_{tool} + (n \times C_{cycle})}{n}$ | Amortized per-part cost | Break-even analysis vs. conventional tooling |
| $n_{break-even} = \dfrac{C_{AM} - C_{conv}}{C_{conv\_per\_part} - C_{AM\_per\_part}}$ | Where AM tooling cost matches conventional | Strategic decision-making |
| $HRC = f(C, Cr, Mo, V)$ | Hardness depends on alloying elements | Why H13/1.2709 are preferred for direct metal tools |
| $\sigma_{residual} = k \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T \cdot \alpha \cdot E}{1-\nu}$ | Residual stress from thermal gradient | Justifies stress-relief heat treatment |

**Typical shrinkage values (memorize for KTU ESE):**

| Material | Casting Shrinkage |
|---|---|
| Cast iron | **1.0%** |
| Carbon steel | **2.0%** |
| Stainless steel | **2.5%** |
| Aluminum alloys | **1.5%** |
| Brass / Bronze | **1.5%** |
| Tool steel (H13) | **2.0%** |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** BMW, Volkswagen, and Ford use **DMLS-printed H13 conformal-cooled inserts** for dashboard and bumper moldings — cycle time reduced by up to **35%**.
- **Aerospace:** GE Aviation uses **DED-repaired turbine blade dies** instead of scrapping worn tools.
- **Medical:** Patient-specific **surgical guides and jigs** (direct tooling concept) are 3D-printed in polymer for operating rooms.
- **Foundries:** ExOne and Voxeljet **binder-jet sand molds** are used as direct tooling in foundries worldwide for low-to-medium volume iron and steel castings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pattern Shrinkage Calculation (Classic KTU 14-Mark Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Design an SLA pattern for a steel injection mold insert with a final tool cavity dimension of $L_{tool} = 200.00 \text{ mm}$. Given: casting shrinkage of steel = **2.0%**, thermal contraction of pattern from room temperature to burnout temperature (**950 °C**) = **1.2%** (effective linear), and SLA photopolymer post-cure dimensional change = **+0.3%** (expansion). Calculate the *initial SLA pattern dimension* to be modeled in CAD.

### Step 1: Define the total shrinkage equation

The CAD pattern dimension must *over-compensate* for all subsequent contractions, so:

$$
L_{pattern} = L_{tool} \times (1 + S_{cast}) \times (1 + S_{thermal}) \times (1 + S_{SLA})
$$

### Step 2: Substitute numerical values

$$
L_{pattern} = 200.00 \times (1 + 0.020) \times (1 + 0.012) \times (1 + 0.003)
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the multipliers individually

$$
(1 + 0.020) = 1.020
$$

$$
(1 + 0.012) = 1.012
$$

$$
(1 + 0.003) = 1.003
$$

### Step 4: Multiply sequentially

$$
L_{pattern} = 200.00 \times 1.020 = 204.00 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_{pattern} = 204.00 \times 1.012 = 206.448 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_{pattern} = 206.448 \times 1.003 = 207.0673 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5: Final Answer with units and reasoning

$$
\boxed{L_{pattern} \approx 207.07 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Reasoning:** The pattern must be **3.5% larger** than the final tool cavity to compensate for cumulative shrinkage in casting, thermal contraction during burnout, and net photopolymer expansion during SLA post-cure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Break-Even Production Volume Between AM Tooling and Conventional Tooling

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A direct DMLS tooling job costs $C_{AM} = \text{₹}3{,}00{,}000$ and produces a part at $C_{AM\_per\_part} = \text{₹}500$. The conventional steel mold costs $C_{conv} = \text{₹}12{,}00{,}000$ and produces a part at $C_{conv\_per\_part} = \text{₹}200$. Determine the break-even production volume $n_{break-even}$ at which both options yield the same total cost.

### Step 1: Write total cost equations

Total cost of AM tooling approach:

$$
C_{T,AM} = C_{AM} + n \cdot C_{AM\_per\_part}
$$

Total cost of conventional tooling approach:

$$
C_{T,conv} = C_{conv} + n \cdot C_{conv\_per\_part}
$$

### Step 2: Equate the two total costs

$$
C_{AM} + n \cdot C_{AM\_per\_part} = C_{conv} + n \cdot C_{conv\_per\_part}
$$

### Step 3: Isolate $n$

$$
n \cdot C_{AM\_per\_part} - n \cdot C_{conv\_per\_part} = C_{conv} - C_{AM}
$$

$$
n \cdot (C_{AM\_per\_part} - C_{conv\_per\_part}) = C_{conv} - C_{AM}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{C_{conv} - C_{AM}}{C_{AM\_per\_part} - C_{conv\_per\_part}}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute values

$$
n = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000}{500 - 200}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{9{,}00{,}000}{300}
$$

### Step 5: Compute final result

$$
\boxed{n_{break-even} = 3000 \text{ parts}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- For $n < 3000$ parts → **AM direct tooling is more economical.**
- For $n > 3000$ parts → **Conventional steel tooling is more economical.**
- For $n = 3000$ parts → Both options are cost-equivalent.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** Whenever asked *"When is AM tooling preferred over conventional tooling?"*, the answer is *always* the **break-even volume range**, computed exactly as above. The KTU examiner expects to see this 5-step derivation explicitly.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Conformal Cooling Channel — Heat Transfer Justification

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Compare the cooling efficiency of a *straight* drilled channel (conventional tool) with a *conformal* channel (AM tool) for the same mold surface area $A = 100 \text{ cm}^2$. The straight channel maintains surface temperature $T_{s,conv} = 80 \text{ °C}$, while the conformal channel maintains $T_{s,conf} = 50 \text{ °C}$. Molding temperature is $T_{m} = 220 \text{ °C}$. Compute the percentage reduction in required heat removal per cycle.

### Step 1: Heat removed by straight channel

$$
Q_{conv} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_m - T_{s,conv})
$$

Effective: $Q_{conv} \propto (220 - 80) = 140 \text{ °C}$ equivalent

### Step 2: Heat removed by conformal channel

$$
Q_{conf} \propto (220 - 50) = 170 \text{ °C} \text{ equivalent}
$$

### Step 3: Wait — reframe. Conformal cooling is faster, not more heat.

The cycle-time reduction arises from *higher heat flux at constant coolant flow*. Equivalently:

$$
\Delta t_{cycle}(\%) = \left( 1 - \dfrac{t_{conf}}{t_{conv}} \right) \times 100
$$

If $t_{conf} = 18 \text{ s}$ and $t_{conv} = 30 \text{ s}$:

$$
\Delta t_{cycle} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{18}{30} \right) \times 100 = 40\%
$$

### Step 4: Result

$$
\boxed{\Delta t_{cycle} = 40\% \text{ reduction in cooling time}}
$$

This is the principal driver for **Direct AM Tooling** in injection molding — the *cycle time reduction* directly multiplies the throughput of the molding press.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Direct vs. Indirect Tooling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **guaranteed 7-mark sub-question** in KTU ESE. Memorize the rows.

| Evaluation Criterion | Direct Tooling | Indirect Tooling |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of process steps** | 1 (AM only) | 2 (AM + casting/molding) |
| **Lead time** | 1–7 days | 1–4 weeks |
| **Tool material options** | Limited to AM-compatible materials | Wide (any castable alloy) |
| **Achievable tool life** | 100 – 10,000 cycles | 50,000 – 1,000,000+ cycles |
| **Surface finish (as-built)** | Ra 5–20 µm (metals), Ra 0.5–5 µm (polymers) | Depends on casting + finishing |
| **Dimensional accuracy** | ±0.1 mm (metals), ±0.05 mm (polymers) | ±0.2–0.5 mm (post-casting) |
| **Conformal cooling** | ✅ Easily achievable | ❌ Not feasible (cast limitations) |
| **Cost for n ≤ 500 parts** | Lower | Higher |
| **Cost for n ≥ 10,000 parts** | Higher (tool degrades) | Lower (durable tool) |
| **Examples** | DMLS H13 inserts, SLA epoxy molds | QuickCast, FDM+Sand, SLS Investment |
| **KTU Past Year Tag** | `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` | `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Direct vs. Indirect Tooling Workflows

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams map the *process flow architecture* of both tooling strategies. The KTU examiner awards full 7 marks only when the workflow is drawn with *explicit decision nodes and feedback loops*.

### 4.1.1 Direct Tooling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start Tool Design] --> A2[CAD Model of Tool with Conformal Cooling]
    A2 --> A3[Slice and Generate Build File]
    A3 --> A4{Choose AM Process}
    A4 -->|Metal| A5[SLM or DED Build]
    A4 -->|Polymer| A6[SLA or FDM Build]
    A5 --> A7[Stress Relief Heat Treatment]
    A6 --> A8[Post Cure UV Exposure]
    A7 --> A9[Support Removal and CNC Finishing]
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10[Surface Treatment Nitriding or PVD]
    A10 --> A11[Direct Installation in Production Machine]
    A11 --> A12[Production of Final Parts]
    A12 --> A13{Tool Worn Out}
    A13 -->|Yes| A14[Discard or Recycle]
    A13 -->|No| A12
```

### 4.1.2 Indirect Tooling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Start Tool Design] --> B2[CAD Model of Pattern with Shrinkage Allowance]
    B2 --> B3[Choose AM Pattern Process]
    B3 -->|Polymer Pattern| B4[SLA or FDM Build]
    B3 -->|Waxlike Pattern| B5[PolyJet or DLP Build]
    B3 -->|Sand Mold Direct| B6[Binder Jetting of Sand Mold]
    B4 --> B7[Conventional Investment Casting]
    B5 --> B7
    B6 --> B8[Direct Pour into Sand Mold]
    B7 --> B9[Knockout and Shakeout]
    B8 --> B9
    B9 --> B10[Heat Treatment and Hardening]
    B10 --> B11[CNC Machining of Critical Sealing Surfaces]
    B11 --> B12[Polishing and EDM Finishing]
    B12 --> B13[Installation in Production Machine]
    B13 --> B14[Production of Final Parts]
    B14 --> B15{Tool Serviceable}
    B15 -->|Yes| B14
    B15 -->|No| B16[Discard or Refurbish]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Decision Logic for Selecting Tooling Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Production Volume Required] --> C2{Volume Less than 1000 Parts}
    C2 -->|Yes| C3{Tool Geometry Complex with Conformal Cooling}
    C2 -->|No| C4{Material Must be Hard Tool Steel}
    C3 -->|Yes| C5[Choose DIRECT TOOLING]
    C3 -->|No| C6[Choose Polymer Direct Tooling SLA FDM]
    C4 -->|Yes| C7{Lead Time Less than 2 Weeks}
    C4 -->|No| C8[Choose CONVENTIONAL TOOLING]
    C7 -->|Yes| C9[Choose INDIRECT TOOLING with AM Pattern]
    C7 -->|No| C8
    C5 --> C10[Recommended: SLM DMLS in H13]
    C6 --> C11[Recommended: SLA photopolymer]
    C9 --> C12[Recommended: QuickCast or FDM Sand Casting]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparative Architecture (Side-by-Side)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DG[Direct Tooling Architecture]
        D1[CAD Tool] --> D2[AM Build]
        D2 --> D3[Finishing]
        D3 --> D4[Tool in Production]
    end
    subgraph IG[Indirect Tooling Architecture]
        I1[CAD Pattern] --> I2[AM Pattern Build]
        I2 --> I3[Conventional Casting or Molding]
        I3 --> I4[Finishing]
        I4 --> I5[Tool in Production]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schematic Interpretation for KTU:** The core architectural difference is the *presence of a secondary conventional process block* in Indirect Tooling. This single block accounts for the longer lead time, lower geometric freedom, and higher per-tool cost — but also for the *superior tool life* of indirect tools.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question is framed in KTU's *definition-and-application* style: a one-line direct question followed by a precise 3-sentence model answer. Memorize the keyword phrases for full marks.

---

### Question 1: Define Direct Tooling. Give two examples.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Direct Tooling is a Rapid Tooling approach in which the *final, production-ready tool* is manufactured directly using an Additive Manufacturing process, without any subsequent casting or molding step. The AM-built part is fitted directly into the production machine. **Example 1:** DMLS-printed H13 steel injection mold inserts with conformal cooling channels. **Example 2:** SLA-printed photopolymer molds for short-run plastic injection molding of prototypes.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Soft Tooling and Hard Tooling with one example each.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Soft Tooling refers to tools made from *low-melting-point alloys, polymers, or filled resins* intended for short production runs (typically < 5,000 parts), such as SLA-printed epoxy injection molds. Hard Tooling refers to tools made from *hardened tool steels or carbides* intended for long production runs (typically > 50,000 parts), such as SLM-printed H13 steel die-casting inserts. The key distinction is *production volume capacity* and *tool material durability*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Module 4 includes an internal choice. Two fully independent questions are provided below. **Attempt either OR.** Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### ❖ Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks, Internal Choice]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Direct Tooling** approach in Additive Manufacturing. Discuss any **two direct metal tooling processes** with their materials, advantages, and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat schematic, explain the workflow of **SLM-based direct tooling of an injection mold insert** with conformal cooling channels. Quantify the cycle-time benefit using a suitable calculation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Direct Tooling = AM produces the final tool directly, no secondary process required.
2. **Process Selection Logic (1 mark):** Tool material must be AM-compatible (e.g., H13 maraging steel for metals, photopolymer for plastics).
3. **Process 1 — SLM (Selective Laser Melting) (2 marks):**
   - Powder bed fusion of maraging steel (1.2709) or H13 tool steel.
   - Layer thickness 20–50 µm, build volume up to 500 × 500 × 500 mm.
   - **Advantage:** Excellent mechanical properties, fully dense (> 99.5%), enables conformal cooling.
   - **Limitation:** High equipment cost, residual stress requires stress relief, limited build size.
4. **Process 2 — DED (Directed Energy Deposition) (2 marks):**
   - Nozzle deposits metal powder/wire, melted by laser or plasma.
   - **Advantage:** Suitable for large tools, can repair worn tools, multi-material deposition.
   - **Limitation:** Lower resolution than SLM, requires post-machining.
5. **Summary (1 mark):** Direct metal tooling is ideal for low-to-medium volume production with complex cooling geometry.

#### Model Solution (b):

1. **Workflow Schematic (3 marks):** [Draw the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1.1 in your answer sheet — CAD → slice → SLM build → stress relief → CNC finish → install.]
2. **Conformal Cooling Explanation (2 marks):** Cooling channels are designed to follow the contour of the mold cavity, maintaining *uniform temperature distribution* across the cavity surface.
3. **Cycle-Time Calculation (2 marks):** 
   - Conventional cycle time: 30 s
   - Conformal-cooled AM tool cycle time: 18 s
   - 
   $$
   \Delta t_{cycle} = \left(1 - \frac{18}{30}\right) \times 100 = 40\%
   $$

   - Throughput gain per hour: from 120 parts/hr to 200 parts/hr (a **66.7% increase in throughput**).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:**
> - Students *lose 2 marks* by omitting the **stress-relief heat treatment** step in the schematic.
> - Students *lose 1 mark* by writing *"SLM is faster than DED"* — this is incorrect; DED is faster for *large* tools.
> - Always write the **cycle-time reduction as a percentage**, not an absolute value.

---

### ❖ Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks, Internal Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the **Indirect Tooling** approach. Describe the **QuickCast process** as a typical example, with its workflow and applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A steel injection mold cavity of dimension 150 mm is to be produced via **Indirect Tooling using the investment casting route**. The casting shrinkage of steel is 2.0%, the photopolymer post-cure expansion is 0.4%, and the thermal contraction of the pattern from ambient to burnout (1000 °C) is 1.5%. Calculate the **SLA pattern dimension** to be designed in CAD. Discuss the limitations of Indirect Tooling. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Indirect Tooling = AM creates a pattern/master, which is then converted to a tool via conventional casting or molding.
2. **Why Indirect Tooling? (1 mark):** Enables use of conventional tool materials (H13, P20, S7) with full mechanical properties; suited for long production runs.
3. **QuickCast Process — Stepwise (4 marks):**
   - Step 1: Build a *thin-walled, hollow* SLA pattern using a specially formulated burnout photopolymer (Accura® e-Stone or equivalent).
   - Step 2: Attach the pattern to a wax sprue and dip in ceramic slurry to build a ceramic shell.
   - Step 3: Place the ceramic-coated pattern in an autoclave at ~ 150 °C to melt out most of the photopolymer.
   - Step 4: Fire the ceramic shell in a furnace at ~ 1000 °C to burn out residual polymer and sinter the shell.
   - Step 5: Pour molten metal (Al, tool steel) into the pre-heated shell.
   - Step 6: Knock out the shell, machine critical surfaces, and finish.
4. **Applications (1 mark):** Aerospace turbine blade prototypes, automotive short-run die inserts, medical implant casting patterns.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Part 1 — Pattern Dimension Calculation (4 marks):**

$$
L_{pattern} = L_{tool} \times (1 + S_{cast}) \times (1 + S_{thermal}) \times (1 + S_{SLA})
$$

Substitute values:

$$
L_{pattern} = 150 \times (1 + 0.020) \times (1 + 0.015) \times (1 + 0.004)
$$

$$
L_{pattern} = 150 \times 1.020 \times 1.015 \times 1.004
$$

$$
L_{pattern} = 150 \times 1.039612 = 155.94 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{pattern} \approx 155.94 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Part 2 — Limitations of Indirect Tooling (3 marks):**

1. **Longer lead time:** 1–4 weeks vs. 1–7 days for direct tooling.
2. **No conformal cooling:** Cast cooling channels must be straight or near-straight, limiting thermal optimization.
3. **Pattern material limitations:** AM photopolymers must have very low ash content (< 0.01%) to avoid contamination of the casting.
4. **Higher per-tool cost:** Each tool requires pattern fabrication, shell building, casting, and finishing — all of which are labor-intensive.
5. **Shrinkage compensation errors:** Cumulative shrinkage factors (often 4–5 sources) introduce dimensional uncertainty of ±0.3–0.5%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> - Students *lose 3 marks* by **forgetting to include the photopolymer expansion term** $(1 + S_{SLA})$ — they only compute casting shrinkage.
> - Students *lose 2 marks* by **not stating units** ("mm" must follow every numerical answer).
> - The phrase *"QuickCast is a Direct Tooling method"* is a common KTU trap answer — QuickCast is **indirect**, since the SLA pattern is *not* the tool.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this on your exam eve):**

- ✅ Direct Tooling = AM builds the **final tool itself**. No secondary process.
- ✅ Indirect Tooling = AM builds a **pattern/master**. Secondary casting or molding converts it to the tool.
- ✅ Direct Tooling saves time and enables **conformal cooling** (20–40% cycle-time reduction).
- ✅ Indirect Tooling is preferred for **long production runs** (high tool life, conventional tool materials).
- ✅ **SLM, DMLS, DED** = Direct Metal Tooling processes.
- ✅ **SLA, FDM, PolyJet** = Most commonly used for Indirect Tooling *patterns*.
- ✅ **QuickCast, FDM + Sand Casting, SLS Investment Casting** = Examples of Indirect Tooling.
- ✅ **Soft Tooling** = polymers, low-melt alloys → short runs.
- ✅ **Hard Tooling** = tool steels, carbides → long runs.
- ✅ **Pattern Shrinkage Equation:** 
$$
L_{pattern} = L_{tool} \times \prod (1 + S_i)
$$

- ✅ **Break-Even Volume Equation:** 
$$
n_{break-even} = \dfrac{C_{conv} - C_{AM}}{C_{AM\_per\_part} - C_{conv\_per\_part}}
$$

- ✅ Common **shrinkage values** to memorize: Cast iron = 1.0%, Steel = 2.0%, Aluminum = 1.5%, Brass = 1.5%.
- ✅ **H13 and 1.2709 (maraging steel)** are the most widely used direct metal tooling alloys.
- ✅ **Conformal cooling** is the *single biggest advantage* of Direct AM Tooling — memorize the 20–40% cycle-time reduction figure.
- ✅ KTU trap answers to avoid:
  - ❌ "QuickCast is a Direct Tooling method."
  - ❌ "Indirect Tooling enables conformal cooling."
  - ❌ "AM tooling is always cheaper than conventional tooling."
  - ❌ "Soft Tooling is for mass production."
- ✅ Always write the **unit (mm, %, °C, ₹)** after every numerical answer in KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Additive Manufacturing: Biomedical, Manufacturing, Aerospace, Automotive, Electronics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT524 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING AND 3D PRINTING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Applications of Additive Manufacturing: Biomedical, Manufacturing, Aerospace, Automotive, Electronics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Additive Manufacturing (AM) Applications** refer to the diverse, cross-industry deployment of layer-by-layer material addition processes (ISO/ASTM 52900 standard classification) to fabricate functional end-use parts, prototypes, tooling, and biological structures across the **biomedical, manufacturing, aerospace, automotive, and electronics** sectors, leveraging the geometric freedom, mass-customization capability, and minimal-material-waste characteristics of AM. As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** (PERAT524, Module 4), the study of AM applications is structured to demonstrate how the seven AM process categories (Vat Photopolymerization, Material Extrusion, Material Jetting, Binder Jetting, Powder Bed Fusion, Directed Energy Deposition, Sheet Lamination) translate into **industry-specific engineering solutions**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Layer Cake vs. The Carved Statue":** > Traditional manufacturing (subtractive) is like carving a statue out of a marble block — you start with *more* and remove material to reveal the shape. Additive manufacturing is like **building a wedding cake one tier at a time**, where each layer is precisely deposited and fused before the next is added. The revolutionary insight is that AM does not care about the *complexity* of the shape — a hollow lattice bone scaffold costs the same time as a solid block, whereas in carving, complexity costs material, time, and tooling. The intuition behind widespread AM adoption is summarized by the **"Three Freedoms"** that subtractive methods cannot offer: - **Geometric Freedom** — internal lattice channels, topology-optimized organic shapes. - **Time Freedom** — no tooling lead time; a CAD file is the only "tool" needed. - **Material Freedom** — multi-material and graded compositions in a single build. ### 1.3 Industry-Specific Relevance Metrics | Industry | Primary AM Value Driver | Typical Tolerance Achieved | Lead-Time Reduction | |---|---|---|---| | Biomedical | Patient-specific anatomy | ± 0.05 mm to ± 0.1 mm | 60–80% | | Aerospace | Lightweighting via topology optimization | ± 0.05 mm (metal PBF) | 30–50% | | Automotive | Rapid prototyping, low-volume tooling | ± 0.1 mm | 70–90% | | Electronics | Conformal 3D circuits, embedded sensors | ± 0.02 mm (micro-AM) | 40–60% | | General Manufacturing | Tooling, jigs, fixtures, spare parts | ± 0.1 mm | 50–70% | > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Reference:** All AM terminology in this module follows **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021** — *"Additive manufacturing — General principles — Fundamentals and vocabulary"*. The standard defines **7 process categories** and is the universally accepted classification referenced by KTU examiners. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Biomedical Applications The biomedical sector is widely regarded as the **highest-impact application domain** of AM because of the natural fit between patient-specific anatomy and AM's mass-customization capability. **2.1.1 Anatomical Models and Surgical Planning** - Built from **CT/MRI DICOM data** converted to **STL** format. - Used for pre-operative rehearsal in complex surgeries (e.g., conjoined-twin separation, facial reconstruction). - Materials: photopolymer resins (SLA/DLP), PLA/ABS (FDM). **2.1.2 Customized Implants and Prosthetics** - **Cranial implants** — PEEK or Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V) via Selective Laser Melting (SLM). - **Dental crowns and bridges** — Cobalt-Chrome (Co-Cr) via DMLS, zirconia via SLA. - **Orthopedic implants** — hip cups, knee spacers, spinal cages using EBM (Electron Beam Melting). **2.1.3 Tissue Engineering Scaffolds** - Fabricated with **porous lattice architecture** to mimic trabecular bone (porosity 60–80%). - Common lattice topologies: **Gyroid, Schwarz Primitive, Diamond, Voronoi**. - Processes: SLA (with bio-resins), FDM (with PCL/PLA), Bioplotting. **2.1.4 Bioprinting (an emerging subset)** - Uses **bio-inks** (cell-laden hydrogels like alginate, gelatin methacrylate — GelMA). - Process: **Extrusion-based bioprinting** is most common; emerging methods include laser-assisted bioprinting and inkjet. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Regulatory Note for Examiners:** Implantable medical devices must comply with **FDA 21 CFR Part 820** (US) and **ISO 13485** (international). Examiners frequently ask about this compliance chain. ### 2.2 Aerospace Applications Aerospace was among the **first industries to qualify metal AM for flight-critical parts** (EASA, FAA certifications around 2015–2018). **2.2.1 Why Aerospace adopts AM:** - Fuel-cost economics: every 1 kg saved on a commercial airliner saves approximately **3,000–4,000 liters of fuel per year**. - Buy-to-fly ratio improvement from **20:1 (machining) → 1:1 (AM)** for complex parts. **2.2.2 Key Parts and Processes** - **B737 MAX LEAP-1B fuel nozzle** (GE Aviation) — Cobalt-Chrome, DMLS; 19 components consolidated to 1, weight ↓ 25%, 5× lifespan. - **A350 XWB cabin brackets** (Airbus) — Titanium Ti-6Al-4V via SLM; topology-optimized, weight ↓ 30%. - **Rocket engine injectors** (SpaceX Raptor, Relativity Terran) — Inconel via DMLS, internal cooling channels impossible to machine. - **Satellite brackets, antenna waveguides** — Aluminum AlSi10Mg via SLM. **2.2.3 Critical Process Variants for Aerospace** - **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)** is mandatory post-process to eliminate internal porosity (closes micro-voids at ~ 920 °C, 100 MPa for Ti-6Al-4V). - **CT scanning** is used for 100% volumetric inspection. ### 2.3 Automotive Applications The automotive industry uses AM primarily for **prototyping, tooling, and low-volume end-use parts**, with mass-production passenger cars still being injection-moulded. **2.3.1 Application Sub-Domains** - **Concept Models & Styling Prototypes** — SLA/FDM for design reviews (used by BMW, Ford, Tata Motors). - **Sand Casting Patterns & Investment Casting Patterns** — Binder Jetting of silica sand, SLA of resin patterns; reduces pattern lead time from 6–8 weeks to 2–3 days. - **Soft tooling & rapid hard tooling** — 3D-printed inserts for injection moulds using metal PBF. - **End-use parts in low-volume / motorsport** — Bugatti's **8-litre monolithic brake caliper** (Ti-6Al-4V, SLM, 41% lighter than the aluminium equivalent). - **Customized interior components** — Local Motors Strati (world's first 3D-printed car body, 2014). ### 2.4 Electronics Applications Electronics AM is one of the **fastest-growing emerging application domains**, encompassing: **2.4.1 3D Printed Circuits and Antennas** - **Aerosol Jet Printing (AJP)** — nanoparticle silver inks deposited on 3D surfaces. - **Direct Ink Writing (DIW)** — silver-paste extrusion on curved substrates. - Enables **conformal antennas** (curved surfaces, no flat-board constraint). **2.4.2 Embedded Electronics and Multi-Material Printing** - **Multi-material PolyJet** — combines rigid and elastomeric photopolymers to print a complete consumer product shell with integrated gaskets in one build. - **Voxel-level material control** — Stratasys PolyJet achieves up to **14 μm layer height** with multi-property voxels. **2.4.3 PCB Prototyping** - **Conductive filament (FiloCopper, Electrifi)** for FDM printing of trace-embedded prototypes. - **Voltera V-One** — direct PCB printing with solder-paste dispensing. **2.4.4 Semiconductor and MEMS** - **Two-Photon Polymerization (2PP, TPP)** — sub-100 nm feature resolution for MEMS and metamaterials. - **Micro-AM** of conformal heatsinks using SLM with copper. ### 2.5 General Manufacturing Applications (Tooling, Spare Parts, Production) **2.5.1 Rapid Tooling** - **Direct Tooling** — 3D-printed mould inserts (PBF metal, DED) injected directly with polymer. - **Indirect Tooling** — 3D-printed master patterns used in silicone moulding, investment casting. **2.5.2 Jigs, Fixtures, and Assembly Aids** - Mass-customization benefit: every workstation can have an ergonomic, part-specific fixture. - **Volkswagen** reports a 90% time reduction for fixture fabrication using FDM. **2.5.3 Spare Parts on Demand (Digital Inventory)** - Spare parts stored as **CAD files**, not physical inventory. - McKinsey estimates this can reduce global warehousing footprints by 20–30%. **2.5.4 Investment Casting Patterns** - SLA patterns of wax-like photopolymer (e.g., **PolyCast, Visijet Wax**). - Replaces traditional injection-moulded wax patterns — eliminates hard tooling for low-volume castings. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Although the *Applications* module is largely qualitative, the following quantitative formulas are **high-yield for KTU numerical sub-parts** (especially Buy-to-Fly ratio, lattice-relative density, and AM economics). | Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Range | |---|---|---|---| | Buy-to-Fly Ratio (BFR) | $\text{BFR} = \dfrac{m_{\text{raw billet}}}{m_{\text{final part}}}$ | dimensionless | 1:1 (AM) to 20:1 (machining) | | Part Weight Reduction | $\%W = \dfrac{m_{\text{conv}} - m_{\text{AM}}}{m_{\text{conv}}} \times 100$ | % | 20 – 60% (topology optimized) | | Lattice Relative Density | $\bar{\rho} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{lattice}}}{\rho_{\text{bulk solid}}}$ | dimensionless | 0.05 – 0.4 (bone scaffolds) | | AM Process Cost | $C_{\text{AM}} = C_{\text{machine}} \cdot t_{\text{build}} + C_{\text{material}} \cdot m + C_{\text{post}}$ | ₹ / \$ | depends on build time | | Fuel Savings per kg saved | $S = k_{\text{fuel}} \cdot \rho_{\text{fuel}} \cdot L_{\text{flight-cycles}}$ | L/year | ~ 3,000 L/yr per kg (commercial) | | Build Time Estimate | $t_{\text{build}} = \dfrac{H}{h_{\text{layer}}} \cdot \dfrac{A_{\text{part}}}{A_{\text{platform}}} \cdot f_{\text{efficiency}}$ | hours | — | > **Notation:** $m$ = mass, $H$ = part height, $h_{\text{layer}}$ = layer thickness, $A$ = area, $f_{\text{efficiency}}$ = machine overhead factor (typically 1.1 – 1.3). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Buy-to-Fly Ratio (BFR) and AM Economics **Problem:** A Ti-6Al-4V aerospace bracket is conventionally machined from a 4.2 kg billet. The final part weighs 0.7 kg. The same part, additively manufactured via SLM, requires 0.85 kg of powder (including 15% waste powder that is recycled). Calculate the Buy-to-Fly Ratio for both processes and quantify the raw-material savings. **Step 1 — Identify given data** - Billet mass (machining): $m_{\text{billet,conv}} = 4.2$ kg - Final part mass: $m_{\text{part}} = 0.7$ kg - AM powder consumed: $m_{\text{powder}} = 0.85$ kg (15% is recycled/recoverable) **Step 2 — BFR for conventional machining** $$ \text{BFR}_{\text{conv}} = \frac{m_{\text{billet,conv}}}{m_{\text{part}}} = \frac{4.2 \text{ kg}}{0.7 \text{ kg}} = 6.0 $$ So conventionally, **6 kg of titanium is bought for every 1 kg that flies**. **Step 3 — BFR for AM (effective)** The effective "bought" powder in AM is the part mass itself, since unused powder is recycled. $$ \text{BFR}_{\text{AM}} = \frac{m_{\text{part}}}{m_{\text{part}}} = 1.0 $$ (Even if 0.85 kg is the powder lot, the recycled fraction returns to inventory and is not lost.) **Step 4 — Raw-material savings** $$ \Delta m = m_{\text{billet,conv}} - m_{\text{powder,lot}} = 4.2 - 0.85 = 3.35 \text{ kg per part} $$ $$ \%\text{Savings} = \frac{3.35}{4.2} \times 100 = 79.76\% $$ **Step 5 — Cost implication (Titanium ≈ ₹ 3,500/kg, indicative)** - Conventional raw cost: $4.2 \times 3500 = \text{₹ } 14{,}700$ - AM raw cost: $0.85 \times 3500 = \text{₹ } 2{,}975$ - Raw material savings: **₹ 11,725 per part (≈ 80%)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Note:** Always state the *effective* BFR for AM as 1:1 even if total powder purchased is higher, because AM powder is **recyclable** (re-used up to 20–30 cycles for Ti-6Al-4V) — this is a key distinction that students often miss. --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Lattice Scaffold Porosity vs. Effective Mechanical Stiffness For a body-centred cubic (BCC) lattice scaffold with strut diameter $d$ and unit cell length $L$: **Step 1 — Relative density formula** $$ \bar{\rho} = \frac{\rho_{\text{lattice}}}{\rho_{\text{solid}}} = \frac{N_{\text{struts}} \cdot \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot L}{L^{3}} = \frac{N_{\text{struts}} \cdot \pi d^{2}}{4 L^{2}} $$ For a BCC unit cell: $N_{\text{struts}} = 8$ (half-struts) → effective $= 4$ full struts per cell. $$ \bar{\rho}_{\text{BCC}} = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{L^{2}} $$ **Step 2 — Numerical example** Let $d = 0.4$ mm, $L = 1.0$ mm (target: cancellous-bone-like porosity). $$ \bar{\rho}_{\text{BCC}} = \frac{\pi (0.4)^{2}}{(1.0)^{2}} = \pi \times 0.16 = 0.503 $$ That is **50.3% relative density** → **49.7% porosity**. For cancellous bone target (~ 30% relative density), reduce $d$ to: $$ d = L \sqrt{\frac{\bar{\rho}}{\pi}} = 1.0 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.30}{3.1416}} = 0.309 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 3 — Gibson-Ashby stiffness relation** $$ \frac{E^{*}}{E_{\text{s}}} = C \cdot \left( \bar{\rho} \right)^{n} $$ Where $C \approx 1$, $n \approx 2$ for open-cell foams. $$ \frac{E^{*}}{E_{\text{s}}} = (0.30)^{2} = 0.09 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; E^{*} \approx 9\% \text{ of solid} $$ This **matches cancellous bone's modulus** (E ≈ 0.1 – 1 GPa vs. cortical bone 15 GPa), which is critical for **stress-shielding avoidance** in orthopedic implants. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters clinically:** If an implant is *too stiff*, it "steals" mechanical load from surrounding bone → bone resorption → implant loosening. AM lattices allow modulus-matching to host bone. --- ### 3.3 Derivation — Build-Time Estimation for a Patient-Specific Cranial Implant **Step 4 — Build time for a hemispherical PEEK cranial implant, 100 mm diameter, 4 mm shell, built via FDM with 0.2 mm layer height.** Number of layers: $$ N_{\text{lay}} = \frac{H}{h_{\text{layer}}} = \frac{4 \text{ mm}}{0.2 \text{ mm}} = 20 \text{ layers} $$ Part plan area (approx. of a 100 mm dia. disc): $$ A_{\text{part}} = \pi r^{2} = \pi (50)^{2} = 7854 \text{ mm}^{2} $$ Platform area (typical 250 × 250 mm): $$ A_{\text{plat}} = 250^{2} = 62{,}500 \text{ mm}^{2} $$ Build time (assuming 1 implant per platform, deposition rate 10 mm³/s): $$ V_{\text{part}} = 7854 \times 4 = 31{,}416 \text{ mm}^{3} $$ $$ t_{\text{build,ideal}} = \frac{31{,}416}{10} = 3141.6 \text{ s} \approx 52.4 \text{ min} $$ With overhead factor $f_{\text{eff}} = 1.25$ (heat-up, cool-down, toolpath overhead): $$ t_{\text{build,actual}} = 52.4 \times 1.25 \approx 65.5 \text{ min} $$ This is **< 1.5 hours per implant**, vs. **4–6 weeks for conventional custom implant manufacturing** — a ~ 95% lead-time reduction that is often cited in KTU exam answers. models | SLA / DLP | Photopolymer resin | Stratasys J850, Formlabs 3B | | Cranial / dental implants | SLM (DMLS) | Ti-6Al-4V, Co-Cr | EOS M 290 | | Orthopedic implants | EBM | Ti-6Al-4V | Arcam Q20plus | | Bioprinting scaffolds | Extrusion-based bioplotter | GelMA, alginate | Organovo, Cellink | | Aerospace brackets | SLM | Ti-6Al-4V, AlSi10Mg | Airbus A350 XWB | | Rocket engine injectors | DMLS | Inconel 718 | SpaceX Raptor | | Automotive prototypes | SLA / FDM | Resin, ABS, Nylon | BMW, Tesla design studios | | Soft tooling inserts | SLM | Tool steel (MS1, H13) | TRUMPF TruPrint | | Conformal antennas | Aerosol Jet | Silver nano-ink | Optomec, Nano Dimension | | PCB prototypes | DIW / inkjet | Conductive ink/paste | Voltera, BotFactory | | Microfluidic MEMS | TPP (2PP) | IP-S photoresist | Nanoscribe GT2 | | Spare parts on demand | FDM / SLM | Nylon, Steel | Siemens Energy (gas turbines) | | Sand casting patterns | Binder Jetting | Silica sand + binder | Voxeljet VX1000 | | Investment casting patterns | SLA | Castable resin | 3D Systems ProJet CP-series | | Topology-optimized fixtures | FDM | PETG, Nylon CF | Volkswagen (VW, Wolfsburg) | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake (Examiner's Pitfall):** Students often confuse **EBM (Electron Beam Melting)** with **SLM (Selective Laser Melting)**. EBM uses an electron beam in vacuum (faster, higher temp, rougher surface) — used for **orthopedic Ti implants**; SLM uses a fiber laser in inert gas (finer resolution) — used for **aerospace brackets**. Examiners frequently test this distinction. ## 4. HIP collapses these voids to < 0.01%, restoring fatigue life to wrought-equivalent levels. ### 4.4 Block Diagram — AM Digital Inventory vs. Physical Inventory ```mermaid flowchart TB Q1[Conventional Workflow] Q2[Physical Spare Part Warehouse] Q3[Slow Lead Time 6 to 12 Weeks] Q4[High Inventory Cost] Q5[Stockout Risk] R1[AM Digital Inventory] R2[CAD File in Cloud] R3[Print on Demand 24 to 72 hours] R4[Low Storage Cost] R5[On Demand Stockout Recovery] Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3 --> Q4 Q4 --> Q5 R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 R4 --> R5 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** "Spare parts on demand" using AM is often cited in KTU Part-B questions under the *General Manufacturing* application sub-module. The trade-off is that AM part cost is **high per piece** but **zero inventory cost** — a break-even analysis is the typical KTU question pattern. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** *List any three biomedical applications of additive manufacturing. Name the AM process used in each.* **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):** 1. **Patient-specific anatomical models** for surgical planning — built using **SLA / DLP** with photopolymer resins from CT/MRI data. 2. **Customized orthopedic and dental implants** (cranial plates, hip cups, dental crowns) — built using **SLM (DMLS)** in **Ti-6Al-4V** or **Co-Cr** alloys. 3. **Tissue engineering scaffolds** with controlled porosity (60–80%) — built using **FDM** with biopolymers like **PCL / PLA**, or **SLA with bio-resins**. > [!TIP] > Examiners award the third mark only if the *process* is correctly paired with the *material* and *application* — generic answers score partial. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]** *What is "Buy-to-Fly Ratio" (BFR)? Why is it significantly lower in additive manufacturing compared to conventional machining for aerospace parts?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Buy-to-Fly Ratio is the ratio of the *raw material purchased* to the *final part mass*. Mathematically, $\text{BFR} = m_{\text{raw billet}} \div m_{\text{final part}}$. - **Conventional scenario (1 mark):** In machining a complex aerospace bracket, BFR is typically **5:1 to 20:1** because ~ 80–95% of the expensive titanium billet becomes chips. - **AM scenario (1 mark):** In PBF processes, BFR is **1:1** (or close to it) because only the powder fused into the part is consumed; unused powder is **sieved and recycled** for subsequent builds, drastically reducing raw-material cost. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Module 4 Pitfalls):** > 1. Students often *describe* applications without naming the **specific AM process** — examiners deduct 2–3 marks per question for missing process names. > 2. Biomedical answers are often written in *lay terms*; examiners expect **biomaterial names** (Ti-6Al-4V, PEEK, PCL) and **regulatory references** (ISO 13485, FDA 21 CFR). > 3. Aerospace answers frequently omit the **post-processing chain** (HIP, CT, surface finish) — this is treated as a 3-mark sub-question in KTU rubrics. > 4. Electronics applications confuse **Aerosol Jet Printing** with **FDM** of conductive filaments — these are *two distinct process families*; the examiner deducts if conflated. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Biomedical + Manufacturing Applications** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Discuss the role of additive manufacturing in **biomedical engineering**, covering anatomical models, customized implants, and tissue engineering scaffolds. For each, name the process, material, and one key advantage. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** *Anatomical Models (2 marks):* - Process: **SLA / DLP**; Material: **photopolymer resin** (e.g., MED610 by Stratasys). - Source: **CT/MRI DICOM** data segmented in software like **Mimics** or **3D Slicer**, exported as **STL**. - Advantage: Enables **pre-operative rehearsal** for complex surgeries (e.g., conjoined-twin separation, liver transplant), reducing intra-operative time by 30–60% and improving surgical confidence. *Customized Implants (3 marks):* - Process: **SLM (Selective Laser Melting)** or **EBM (Electron Beam Melting)**; Material: **Ti-6Al-4V** (for load-bearing), **Co-Cr** (for dental). - Process variants: **Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS)** by EOS for dental bridges; **EBM by Arcam** for hip cups (faster build, slightly rougher surface ~ Ra 25 μm, removed by post-machining). - Key advantage: **Patient-specific geometry** matched to CT-derived anatomy; **porous lattice interfaces** for **osseointegration** (bone ingrowth into implant). - Regulatory compliance: **ISO 13485** + **FDA 21 CFR Part 820** mandatory for implantables. *Tissue Engineering Scaffolds (2 marks):* - Process: **FDM** with **PCL/PLA** or **SLA with bio-resins**; emerging: **extrusion-based bioprinting** with **GelMA** bio-inks. - Design: Lattice topologies (**Gyroid, Schwarz Primitive**) with **60–80% porosity** and pore size **200–500 μm** for cell migration. - Advantage: **Modulus-matching** to host bone avoids stress-shielding-induced resorption (Gibson-Ashby relation). --- **(b)** Explain the concept of **rapid tooling** and **spare parts on demand** in general manufacturing applications of AM. Compare direct and indirect tooling with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** *Rapid Tooling — Concept (1 mark):* Rapid tooling is the use of AM to produce moulds, dies, patterns, or fixtures in **days rather than weeks**, eliminating the long lead time of conventional tool-room machining. *Direct Tooling (3 marks):* - The 3D-printed part **itself** acts as the mould/die. - Example: **Conformal cooling channels** in injection-mould inserts — SLM-built in **H13 tool steel** with internal channels that follow the contour of the cavity. Result: **40% cycle-time reduction** and **15–20% warpage reduction** in moulded plastic parts. - Suitable for **low-to-medium volume production** (1,000 – 50,000 parts). *Indirect Tooling (2 marks):* - The 3D-printed part is a **master pattern** used to create a secondary tool. - Example: SLA pattern → **silicone mould** for vacuum casting of polyurethane prototypes (room-temperature vulcanizing silicone, RTV). - Example: SLA "wax" pattern → **investment casting** of metal prototypes. - Suitable for **prototypes and very low volumes** (1 – 100 parts). *Spare Parts on Demand (1 mark):* - Spare parts stored as **CAD files in cloud** rather than physical inventory. - McKinsey estimates **20–30% warehousing cost reduction** for industries with large legacy SKUs (e.g., Siemens Energy printing gas-turbine blades on demand). > **Examiner Tip:** Marks are awarded for *naming examples* (Siemens, EOS, H13) — abstract answers lose 1–2 marks. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Aerospace + Automotive + Electronics Applications** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** With suitable examples, describe the applications of additive manufacturing in the **aerospace industry**. Explain why post-processing (HIP, CT inspection) is critical for flight-qualified parts. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** *Why Aerospace adopts AM (2 marks):* - **Weight reduction** is paramount — every kg saved on a commercial airliner saves ~ **3,000 L of fuel/year** (~ ₹ 2.5 lakh/year/kg). - **Buy-to-fly ratio** drops from 5:1–20:1 (machining) to **1:1** (PBF). - **Design freedom** — topology optimization and internal cooling channels impossible to machine. - **Parts consolidation** — 19 parts in the LEAP fuel nozzle consolidated to **1 part** (GE Aviation). *Application Examples (3 marks):* 1. **Airbus A350 XWB cabin brackets** — Ti-6Al-4V SLM, 30% lighter. 2. **GE LEAP fuel nozzle** — Co-Cr DMLS, 25% lighter, 5× lifespan. 3. **SpaceX Raptor rocket injectors** — Inconel 718 DMLS, internal regenerative cooling channels. 4. **Boeing 787 satellite antenna waveguides** — AlSi10Mg SLM, RF-optimized topology. *Post-Processing — HIP & CT (2 marks):* - As-built PBF parts have **0.1–0.5% residual porosity** (gas pockets from melt-pool dynamics). - **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)**: parts heated to ~ 920 °C, pressurized to ~ 100 MPa in argon; micro-voids collapse → porosity < 0.01% → fatigue life equivalent to wrought alloy. - **CT (Computed Tomography) inspection**: 100% volumetric non-destructive testing detects internal defects undetectable by surface inspection. - Regulatory bodies (**FAA, EASA**) require HIP + CT for flight-critical parts, per **AMS 7000-series** specifications. --- **(b)** Discuss the applications of AM in the **automotive and electronics** industries. Highlight at least **two examples per industry** with the process and material used. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** *Automotive (3.5 marks):* 1. **Concept models and styling prototypes** — SLA (resin) and FDM (ABS, Nylon) used by **BMW, Ford, Tesla** for design review 6–8 weeks faster than clay modelling. 2. **Investment casting patterns** — **SLA wax-like resin** (e.g., 3D Systems Visijet Wax) for low-volume metal components (turbocharger housings, gearbox prototypes). 3. **Soft tooling for short-run injection moulding** — **SLM inserts in P-20 or H13 tool steel** for production runs of 1,000 – 50,000 parts. 4. **End-use parts in motorsport** — **Bugatti 8-litre brake caliper**, Ti-6Al-4V SLM, monolithic, 41% lighter than the aluminium version. 5. **Local Motors Strati** (2014) — world's first 3D-printed car body, FDM with carbon-fibre-reinforced ABS. *Electronics (3.5 marks):* 1. **Conformal antennas on 3D surfaces** — **Aerosol Jet Printing (AJP)** with **silver nanoparticle ink** on curved substrates (used in 5G smartphone antennas and aerospace radomes). 2. **Multi-material 3D electronics enclosures** — **Stratasys PolyJet** with rigid + elastomeric photopolymers + embedded conductive traces; integrates gaskets and connectors in one build. 3. **PCB prototyping** — **Direct Ink Writing (DIW)** of silver paste, or **FDM with conductive filaments** (e.g., Electrifi, FiloCopper) for trace-embedded boards. 4. **MEMS and metamaterials** — **Two-Photon Polymerization (TPP / 2PP)** with sub-100 nm resolution using **IP-S photoresist** (Nanoscribe GT2) for micro-lenses, photonic crystals, and lab-on-chip devices. 5. **Conformal 3D heatsinks for chips** — **SLM with pure copper** (TRUMPF TruPrint) for optimized fin geometry impossible to machine. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 4 — Applications of AM: 10 Key Takeaways for KTU Exam** 1. **Five primary application sectors** — Biomedical, Aerospace, Automotive, Electronics, General Manufacturing. 2. **Biomedical = patient-specific** — driven by DICOM → STL pipeline; dominant processes are SLA (models), SLM/EBM (implants in Ti-6Al-4V), FDM (scaffolds in PCL/PLA). 3. **Aerospace = lightweighting + Buy-to-Fly** — BFR of 1:1 is the headline metric; HIP + CT are *mandatory* post-processing for flight-critical parts. 4. **Automotive = prototyping + tooling** — FDM/SLA for prototypes, SLM for tooling inserts, with motorsport being the main adopter of end-use AM parts. 5. **Electronics = emerging domain** — Aerosol Jet, PolyJet, DIW, and TPP are the key process families; conformal antennas and 3D PCBs are the flagship applications. 6. **General Manufacturing = digital inventory** — AM enables spare-parts-on-demand; CAD-as-inventory reduces warehousing cost by 20–30%. 7. **Lattice scaffolds** are the bridge between AM and tissue engineering — Gibson-Ashby relation $E^{*} = C \cdot \bar{\rho}^{n}$ governs modulus-matching. 8. **Regulatory frameworks** — ISO/ASTM 52900 (process), ISO 13485 (medical), AMS 7000 (aerospace), FDA 21 CFR Part 820 (US medical). 9. **Common exam traps** — confusing EBM with SLM; confusing AJP with FDM-conductive-filament; missing process names in answers. 10. **High-yield numericals** — BFR calculation, lattice relative density, build-time estimation, and weight-reduction percentage using $\%W = \dfrac{m_{\text{conv}} - m_{\text{AM}}}{m_{\text{conv}}} \times 100$. > [!TIP] > **Final Revision Strategy:** Memorize the **Process-Material-Application** triple for at least *one example in each of the five sectors*. Examiners award full marks only when all three are present and correctly linked.
